speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your thoughts about a message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the most important part is to have a healthy family life. I think if-- if you have that, you can overcome any kind of adversity because the families helps each other. They stay together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other thing, of course, that's more for the United States to be vigilant, to maintain a democratic form of government that no dictator ever has a chance to-- to rule this country, to-- to stay free. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And say no to drugs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Learn a profession-- learn a trade-- with which you can always make a living. Doesn't have-- you don't have to be a lawyer, doctor. If you-- if you-- if you have a trade or profession, you're good at it, you make a good living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So life was good to me with my trade. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] I think that's really my message, if it-- if it is a message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Matzdorff, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, we thank you for sharing your story with us, and to share with the future generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Matzdorff, can you tell us who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture, I believe, was taken on our balcony in-- in Berlin. There standing in the background is my dad. To the left, the gentleman without hair is my grandfather Otto. And [LAUGHS] the foreground it's me when I was about six month old or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name, in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Herbert.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my grandfather, Alex Rothe, R-O-T-H-E, and my grandmother, Adele. And I believe this was in the early '20s, strolling on the-- on a vacation in the Baltic Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: The previous picture, what country in the Baltic Sea was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- in Germany, on the Baltic Sea and they-- they were parents of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this photograph shows, on the right, my dad, and myself when I was about, oh, maybe 16, 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that would have been 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city this was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Matzdorff, tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the infamous Kennkarte, which every Jew had to carry on his person to be identified. And I believe that was in early 1938 or March, '38, as this one was issued. That we all had to carry in order to-- if we're stopped on the streets, they're to identify that we're Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you carried this in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: You carry-- in Berlin, yes. Carried that on-- on us in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my actual traveling passport, which were given to leave the-- the country and-- and to enter Shanghai. And that was very important to have that to-- to be able to get off the ship in Shanghai. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was issued in-- shortly before we left in 1939, I think about March, April. March of 1939. Shows my profession and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it also has a-- the J on the front page, identifying us as Jews. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] You know, it's-- It's-- it's important if you can zoom in on this writing here, because what-- what it says, it's a-- it's an unbedenklichkeitsbescheinigung for my father, my mother, and myself, which means that my dad does not owe any money to anybody, not even to the government, and that the so-- so-called [? Judenbusse, ?] which in our case was-- was not raised because my parents turned in all their jewelry as the-- as payment for the [? Judenbusse ?] after the Crystal Night, which it shows right there on the bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this document required for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was-- was necessary in order to obtain your exit visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was issued 4th of April of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the city of Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. And it's issued by the police department. On the upper left-hand corner, it shows that it's issued by the police department. This is a picture of Mrs. Ziska in whose house we stayed the day of the-- of the Crystal Night, with an inscription on the back to remember the-- the hours and days that we spent with them during that period, where we stayed-- my dad and I, we stayed there for two weeks. Her name is Mrs. Ziska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. Oh, yes. Tutti. Tutti Ziska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tutti Ziska. And this picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- the picture was given to me shortly before we left in April, I believe, '39. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I-- I-- I kept it all these years. This picture shows our family onboard ship of the SS on the way to Shanghai. On the far right is my grandmother Adele. Then with the sailor hat is my dad, Herbert, my mom, Edith, next to him, and myself with a tropical helmet. And standing next to an Indian gentleman who was a passenger on board. And I believe-- I believe his name was [? Opal. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What month and year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in April of 1939. And the Indian gentleman was on his way to Bombay. This plaque was-- and many others like it were-- installed by the Japanese authorities on lamp posts throughout the borders of the district in Honkew in Shanghai, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the ghetto was started in '42, '43, which did not permit us to leave the area. This particular plaque is an original which I personally tore off on the morning of the-- the end of the war and had it in my possession ever since. This is a collage which was made up by my daughter-in-law, Shari, showing [? Marianne and ?] myself at a wedding picture, together with the invitation that were printed up for our wedding in March 30, 1947, in Shanghai. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This document is our marriage certificate issued by the Jewish Community Association in Shanghai, actually showing that Marianne and I really got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was issued in-- well, our wedding was-- was March 30, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and the Hebrew date is on there, too. This picture was taken in Minneapolis, Minnesota. And it shows-- oh, and let's see, the year was in 1950. Shows on the far left my son Steve, then myself. Then next to me is my wife, Marianne, then my daughter Beverly, my mother, Edith, and my dad on the far right, Herbert. This is one of our family pictures, taken in Hanukkah of 1990. Standing from left to right is my daughter Shirley Matzdorff, who now lives in New York and unfortunately couldn't be here today. Next is my son Jeffrey Matzdorff, still single, both of them. Myself, Gary Matzdorff, and to the right of me is my son Steve Matzdorff. And on the far right, Mitchell Golding, my son-in-law. Seated is-- on the left-- is my daughter-in-law Shari Matzdorff, who is married to Steve. And next to her is Beverly Matzdorff, married to Mitchell. And seated on her lap is our granddaughter, Amanda. And next to Amanda is my mom, Edith Matzdorff, who passed away in 1993. And next to her is my wife, Marianne Matzdorff, who unfortunately could not be here today because she's in a hospital and hopefully should be coming home in a day or two. And she is getting much better. She's recovering from an infection. And seated in the front row is our granddaughter Heather Matzdorff, and next to her is Chuck, or Charles, Matzdorff-- Chucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was this taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in my son's home in-- in Studio City. This document shows the-- the depth-- the death of my grandparents, Otto Matzdorff and Sarah Matzdorff. I requested this document. It was issued in Theresienstadt, Czechoslovakia, on one of my visits. And eventually they mailed this to me, showing exactly when my grandparents were transported from Breslau, and which transport, which, even, car they were in on the way to Theresienstadt, and when they died. So at least I have some document showing when-- [CRYING] when they were killed or died. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you receive this document?

speaker: interviewer
text: In person when you were in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was in-- at-- at the Theresienstadt Concentration Camp with my family. And-- but the person who issues these documents was not there at the moment, so I left some money for them and asked them to mail it to me, and they mailed it to me eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Matzdorff, could you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To my far right is my son-in-law, Mitchell Golding. and next to him is son Jeffrey Matzdorff. And our granddaughter Amanda Golding, who is the daughter of Beverly Matzdorff Golding. And in front of me is Heather Matzdorff, our granddaughter, Steve's daughter. And next to me is my son Steve Matzdorff. And to his left is Chuck Matzdorff, the son of Steve and Shari. And seated on the far left is Shari Matzdorff, our daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mitch, we'll start with you and tell us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few of your thoughts about your father-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, going back, you know, I've heard some of the stories that-- that Gary and Marianne, my father-in-law and mother-in-law, have experienced. And basically I'm just very proud of them. Obviously they had some very turmoil and hard conditions when they were in-- interned in Shanghai and obviously came to this country with basically nothing and have-- have made themselves their own business and have been very successful in-- in everything that they have done, monetarily along with their children and everything. And just very-- just very proud to-- to be associated with them, and I think that they've done a great job under those conditions, along with all the other survivors of that era. And not really anything else, really, to-- to say, but just glad to be part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few short words, and that is that I think that the hardships that both of our parents went through during those years has been probably one of the biggest reasons why our family is so strong and-- and has-- we work together. The-- all four kids work together, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Matzdorff strength has endured a lot and will always endure. We're just a very strong family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beverly?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's being a Matzdorff, too, by the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's being a Matzdorff. [LAUGHTER] I love my parents very much. I love my family very much, and I'm proud for them, for what they did and how they survived, and for all the rest of the survivors. And we should never forget what happened and continue to tell the stories [SOBBING] that we learned from our parents and from our grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amanda? Is there something you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who do you love?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who do you love?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandma and grandpa. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Steve?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I'd like to say that there is one Matzdorff missing, and that's our-- two. There's, of course, Mom, who, you know, I'm sure you've already talked about. But we also have another sister, Shirley, who is living in New York at the time-- at this time. And she is, of course, an integral part of our family and our business. And Shirley, you know, if you ever see the video, just to let you know we're thinking about you. What-- what impresses me-- excuse me, Heather. I'm sorry for my foot here, but I can't sit like this for any great length of time. What impresses me about this-- this-- the ordeal that our parents went through was how their parents reacted to what was happening and how our grandfather-- our paternal grandfather-- didn't feel the need, initially, to leave. And how our grandmother, Edith-- that was Herbert-- and our grandmother, Edith, who pushed to get the family out of Germany. And how they almost didn't get out, how they couldn't get out after Crystal Night, and how they were fortunate to get out in-- in '39. And similarly, what-- what my mother's family went through with Grandpa Leo getting locked up in Austria. It's-- it's amazing that they are survivors, that they didn't go through the-- the concentration camp experience. They experienced the Holocaust in a different fashion, and I think that's what's amazing. That's what binds them. It's what binds us. But it's what binds them to-- to the Holocaust, but fortunately through a-- a better outcome than so many others. We're fortunate. We're fortunate that-- that they're scarred but not-- not terminally scarred. And memories are still very good, and that our parents were able to go back to Europe and go-- go back and-- and see German friends and Austrian friends. That's what makes this so great.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's-- it's-- it's-- we-- we have lived it all our lives. We are the-- the Matzdorff children, as the Shanghaiers call us, and we are-- we are survivors too, in-- in a sense because we've grown up with the memories, and-- and have grown up with the stories. And I think we are of a different ilk because of it. It's-- it's fantastic that we have the opportunity to be here today to-- to share with you our feelings about it, and just very proud to be a Jew and very proud to be an American and very proud of our collective families. Thank you. Shari, we'll go to you next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I, too, am very proud to be a member of the family. And I think if-- if one thing I've learned from my experience over the last 19-- almost 19 years of being married is that the Matzdorff family is a very strong family. And they have instilled in me, which has helped me instill in my children, I hope, a sense of family a sense of tradition, of the Jewish people. The growth going forward with-- with Judaism and keeping hold of-- of what is important as family, as-- as Jews. And I come from a-- a very Heinz 57 kind of family, my-- you know, my side of the family. And this has really brought me in touch with-- with family and-- and feelings that I never experienced. And I know my children feel that, and they feel a part of something that's-- that's bigger-- that's bigger than we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Chuck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I speak for my sister when I say this-- I'm-- I'm very proud of my family. And I-- I think that, had it not been for my grandma and grandpa, Heather and I wouldn't be here and the rest of this family wouldn't be here. I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Matzdorff, are there any final thoughts you'd like to add to be included?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I just-- I love my family and sorry that my wife couldn't be here. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] But she'll be home soon. I know she loves the family. [CRYING] But she'll be well. She'll be here soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once again, thank you for sharing your story of survival with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
